coredata.dc:title: Charge transfer dynamics in graphene quantum dot-porphyrin derivative nanohybrids appraised via experimental and theoretical calculations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Pallikkara, Athira
Vijay, Pothoppurathu M
Ramakrishnan, Kala
Jayasree, Elambalassery G
coredata.dc:description: This article is designed to unveil the electronic and spectroscopic property changes of electron withdrawing/releasing group substituted porphyrins, in porphyrin-graphene quantum dot nanohybrids via experimental and computational calculations. Herein, amino, methyl, fluoro & nitro derivatives of meso substituted Tetraphenylporphyrin referred as TAPP, TMPP, TFPP & TNPP respectively, are synthesized from the corresponding benzaldehydes. Charge transfer dynamics of their nanohybrid systems with graphene quantum dots are investigated by electronic spectroscopy, steady state fluorescence and lifetime decay analysis. Negative Gibb's free energy change and dynamic quenching constants stipulate a photoinduced electron transfer with dynamic quenching mechanism within the nanohybrids under scrutiny. Further the computational calculations with the aid of optimized geometries, scatter plots and energy decomposition analysis reflect the same experimental observations. Both experimental and computational studies show that the electron withdrawing group substituted porphyrins have higher interaction with GQD than the electron releasing group substituted porphyrins.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porphyrins
Graphene quantum dots
Non-covalent interactions
Density functional theory
Steady state luminescence
Photophysics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: 2.4 Photophysical studies of GQDs and porphyrin derivative nanohybrids
Charge transfer dynamics between porphyrin derivatives and GQDs
Computational studies on the interactions within GQD-porphyrin nanohybrid systems

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We thank Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the support through SMNRI Project and STIC & Department of Physics for the analysis. We also acknowledge DST-SERB for the financial support (EMR/2016/005601). The partial financial support obtained from TDP/BDTD/34/2021, UGC-SAP, DST-FIST, DST-PURSE, PLEASE and RUSA projects are also acknowledged. The authors would also like to thank the computer center at the Center of Integrated Studies, CUSAT for providing the computational facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Visible absorption spectra and (B) Steady state fluorescence spectra of porphyrin derivatives under study in acetonitrile solvent. Fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength equal to wavelength of corresponding Soret bands in spectrum (A).
UV–Visible absorption spectra of (A) TAPP, (B) TMPP, (C) TNPP and (D) TFPP in acetonitrile on the incremental addition of GQD from 0 to 170 μg.

Steady state fluorescence spectra of (A) TAPP, (B) TMPP, (C) TNPP & (D) TFPP in acetonitrile on the incremental addition of GQD from 0 to 170 μg recorded at an excitation wavelength of 425, 415, 419 & 413 nm respectively.

Time-resolved emission decay traces of (A) TAPP, (B) TMPP, (C) TNPP & (D) TFPP on the incremental addition of GQD from 0 to 170 μg, with an emission wavelength of 680, 653, 651 & 649 nm respectively, using 340 nm light emitting diode as the source.
Stern-Volmer plot corresponding to the changes in fluorescence and lifetime values of porphyrin derivatives by the incremental addition of GQDs.
Frontier molecular orbitals of TAPP, TMPP, GQD, TNPP and TFPP obtained by DFT method using CAM-B3LYP/6-31g(d,p) basis set.
Frontier molecular orbitals of the four porphyrin based hybrid systems with GQD.
(A) Electrostatic potential energy, (B) sum of steric and quantum energies and (C) total energy of nanohybrids calculated by Shubin Liu's decomposition analysis.
Absorbance and emission peaks of four porphyrin derivatives in acetonitrile solvent.
Electronic energy levels of porphyrin derivatives and GQD obtained via Cyclic Voltammetry and optical bandgap.

head.ce:title: Charge transfer dynamics in graphene quantum dot-porphyrin derivative nanohybrids appraised via experimental and theoretical calculations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi 682 022, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Applied Chemistry, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi 682 022, Kerala, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Inter University Center for Nanomaterials and Devices, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi 682 022, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Inter University Center for Nanomaterials and Devices
Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bInter University Center for Nanomaterials and Devices, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Kochi 682 022, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This article is designed to unveil the electronic and spectroscopic property changes of electron withdrawing/releasing group substituted porphyrins, in porphyrin-graphene quantum dot nanohybrids via experimental and computational calculations. Herein, amino, methyl, fluoro & nitro derivatives of meso substituted Tetraphenylporphyrin referred as TAPP, TMPP, TFPP & TNPP respectively, are synthesized from the corresponding benzaldehydes. Charge transfer dynamics of their nanohybrid systems with graphene quantum dots are investigated by electronic spectroscopy, steady state fluorescence and lifetime decay analysis. Negative Gibb's free energy change and dynamic quenching constants stipulate a photoinduced electron transfer with dynamic quenching mechanism within the nanohybrids under scrutiny. Further the computational calculations with the aid of optimized geometries, scatter plots and energy decomposition analysis reflect the same experimental observations. Both experimental and computational studies show that the electron withdrawing group substituted porphyrins have higher interaction with GQD than the electron releasing group substituted porphyrins.

Nanohybrid systems are of great importance for the efficient charge transfer dynamics hindering the electron recombination process in photovoltaic applications.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Four pyrrole rings fused core habitually known as porphyrin is a trump card among the photoactive materials. High molar extinction coefficients, thermal and photostability, as well as ease of functionalization due to the availability of additional sites, are just a few of these molecule's fascinating characteristics [1]. Additionally, the absorption of porphyrins can span the maximum of ultraviolet – visible spectral region when joined with various molecules or ions. During these times, this kind of materials found worth in different fields like solar energy harvesting and storage [2,3,4], photodynamic therapy [5,6,7], optoelectronic devices [8,9,10,11], sensors [12], catalysis [13,14,15], fuel cells[16,17] and so on. Likewise, the research on highly photostable materials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) show an exponential growth in this decade because of its excellent optoelectronic properties, aqueous solubility, less toxicity, profuse surface modifications, high carrier mobility, large surface area and bio-compatibility [18,19]. Due to these peculiarities of GQDs, it is important to note its interactions with electron withdrawing/releasing groups substituted porphyrins. Researches on hybrid systems of porphyrin and nanoparticles like GQDs are mounting during these years.

Literature reports on porphyrin and inorganic quantum dots (QDs) are very common, whereas with GQDs are rare. Generally, tetracarboxylic acid functionalized porphyrins are studied along with inorganic/organic QDs and they behave as charge donors in most of the cases [20]. Even though, it may get varied depending on the system under consideration. Mandal et al. explored the charge transfer dynamics in CdTe QD-Tetrakis(4-carboxyphenyl)porphyrin nanocomposites and demonstrated the hot electron transfers from QD to porphyrin and ultrafast electron transfer in opposite direction [21]. Similarly along with graphene based materials, porphyrins interact through photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [20,22], Energy transfer [23,24], Z-scheme heterojunctions[25] etc. The energy transfer between multi-layer GQDs and porphyrin unimolecular micelle shows high efficiency upto 93.6 % [26]. In general it is observed that, in porphyrin-GQD hybrid systems, the porphyrins act as PET donors and GQDs as acceptors, whereas the reverse is observed in the case of energy transfer. Recent discovery reveals that this kind of secondary/ternary/higher composite systems find possible applications in different areas like photosensitizers, sensing, photodynamic therapy, photovoltaics and so on [2,27,28]. Successful application of such hybrid materials is possible by knowing their carrier transfer dynamics.

Our previous endeavours on the electronic and spectroscopic properties of porphyrins-GQD hybrids unveil some interesting facts leading us to examine the same with electron withdrawing/donating group substituted porphyrin molecules and GQDs. For this study, we have chosen four molecules such as amino (-NH2), methyl (-CH3), nitro (-NO2) and fluoro (-F) derivatives of Tetraphenylporphyrin referred as TAPP, TMPP, TNPP and TFPP respectively. They were characterized by LC-MS, 1H NMR, electronic and fluorescence spectroscopic studies. Later the photophysical properties and electron transfer dynamics of four nanohybrid systems: TAPP:GQD, TMPP:GQD, TFPP:GQD and TNPP:GQD were explored via electronic, steady state fluorescence and lifetime analysis. These experimental results were validated theoretically with density functional theory and Shubin Lu's decomposition analysis. Thus, this article embraces both experimental techniques and computational calculations to unravel the non-covalent interactions within the hybrids at the molecular level and provides insight into strategies for developing organic/inorganic hybrids with improved charge transfer ability.

All the chemicals except pyrrole were used as received from industrial sources without further purification. Pyrrole used for the condensation reaction in the synthesis of porphyrin derivatives was freshly distilled by standard procedures. The Spectro UV–Vis Double beam UVD-3500 spectrometer was used to record the UV–Visible absorption spectra in the range 200–900 nm, in acetonitrile solvent. Steady state fluorescence spectra were recorded using Horiba Fluorolog-3 (FL-1057) fluorescence spectrometer using the same solvent used in UV–Visible spectroscopy. Time-resolved life time analysis was recorded on Horiba DeltaHub using 340 nm nano LED as the source and data analyzed by multi-exponential curve fitting using DAS6 software. Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) measurements were obtained on CHI660E model Instrument (Electrochemical workstation). Bench top ultrasonicator was used for the synthesis of nanocomposites. The LC-MS system of Shimadzu Co. Ltd. (model 8045) with an interface ESI and direct infusion method was used to process the sample.
Graphene Oxide (GO) prepared by modified Hummer's method was used for the synthesis of Graphene Quantum Dots (GQDs) by hydrothermal method [29,30], as in our earlier reports.

The synthesis of porphyrin derivatives (PDs) is shown in scheme S1. Freshly distilled pyrrole and corresponding benzaldehyde with same molar ratio were added to refluxing propionic acid and the reaction was continued for about half an hour. The reaction mixture was cooled down to room temperature and the coloured crystals obtained were filtered out. After filtration, the solid crystals were washed perfectly with methanol and later with hot water. The crystals were dried in vacuum.

5,10,15,20-Tetrafluorophenylporphyrin (TFPP): Yield (24 %); LC-MS (ESI) m/z: [M+], calculated for C44H26F4N4 686.21, found 687.65 (61.29 %); 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3, TMS) δ in ppm: -2.81 (s, 2H, pyrrole NH), 8.86 (s, 8H, pyrrole ring), 8.19, 8.18 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs) & 7.50, 7.49 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs); UV/vis (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 413 (Soret), 510 (QIV), 542 (QIII), 579 (QII), 643 (QI); Emission (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 649, 715.

5,10,15,20-Tetramethylphenylporphyrin (TMPP): Yield (23 %); LC-MS (ESI) m/z: [M+], calculated for C48H38N4 670.31, found 671.65 (48.35 %); 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3, TMS) δ in ppm: -2.78 (s, 2H, pyrrole NH), 8.85 (s, 8H, pyrrole ring), 8.10, 8.08 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs), 7.58, 7.53 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs) & -2.70 (s, 12H, methyl Hs); UV/vis (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 415 (Soret), 514 (QIV), 550 (QIII), 592 (QII), 645 (QI); Emission (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 653, 717.

5,10,15,20-Tetranitrophenylporphyrin (TNPP): Yield (19 %); LC-MS (ESI) m/z: [M+], calculated for C44H26N8O8 794.19, found 795.65 (65.14 %); 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3, TMS) δ in ppm: -2.80 (s, 2H, pyrrole NH), 8.70 (s, 8H, pyrrole ring), 8.42, 8.41 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs), 8.11, 8.10 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs); UV/Vis (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 419 (Soret), 509 (QIV), 546 (QIII), 580 (QII), 643 (QI); Emission (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 651, 715.

5,10,15,20-Tetraaminophenylporphyrin (TAPP): Yield (90 %); LC-MS (ESI) m/z: [M+], calculated for C44H34N8 674.29, found 675.65 (100 %); 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3, TMS) δ in ppm: -2.78 (s, 2H, pyrrole NH), 8.82 (s, 8H, pyrrole ring), 7.93, 7.91 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs), 7.00, 6.98 (d, 8H, phenyl Hs), 3.95 (s, 8H, -NH2); UV/Vis (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 427 (Soret), 524 (QIV), 565 (QIII), 605 (QII), 660 (QI); Emission (CH3CN) λmax, nm: 684, 749.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 2.4 Photophysical studies of GQDs and porphyrin derivative nanohybrids
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between GQDs and porphyrin derivatives were studied by various spectroscopic techniques, by the incremental addition of GQDs to fixed concentration of porphyrins. Ground state interaction study was evaluated using UV–Visible spectroscopy, whereas the excited state interactions were probed by steady state fluorescence and lifetime decay analysis.

Computational calculations were performed using CAM-B3LYP method and 6-31G(d,p) basis set, a quantum mechanical approach for calculating the electronic structure, with the aid of Gaussian 09 program package. Similar to our earlier work [2], we have chosen 13 rings membered GQD with –COOH, –OH & epoxide groups for all its calculations. The GQD used is Lerf-Klinowski based model, which was used by Lerf et al. for graphene oxide and reduced graphene oxide is in correlation with the experimental observations [31]. Meanwhile the molecular geometries of each of the porphyrin derivatives were also designed by the GaussView 5.0 software. The geometries were fully optimized without any symmetry constraints. The bandgap (Eg) was calculated at the same level of theory. Non-covalent systems were analyzed from the optimized geometries of each system. Molecular electrostatic potential surface analysis and ShubinLiu's energy decomposition analysis were conducted on these optimized geometries. Shubin Liu's decomposition analysis approach considers the entire molecular energy as the sum of steric, electrostatic and quantum components. The steric component corresponds to the accurate kinetic energy of the electrons, electrostatic term is the aggregate of all classical coulomb interactions and quantum term corresponds to energy purely by quantum impact (Equantum = EPauli+Exc).

GQDs used for this study was synthesized by hydrothermal method from graphene oxide and are characterized using FESEM, HRTEM, TGA, FT-IR, UV–Visible spectroscopy, steady state fluorescence & lifetime decay analysis, as reported in our earlier work [32,33]. The photophysical characteristics of the GQD that are essential for this study are given in the supporting information (Fig. S1(A), S1(B) & S2).

The Alder method, which is the conventional method of porphyrin synthesis was used to synthesize four porphyrin derivatives, such as the one containing electron donating groups like -NH2 & -CH3 and electron withdrawing groups like -NO2 & -F labelled as Tetraaminophenylporphyrin (TAPP), Tetramethylphenylporphyrin (TMPP), Tetranitrophenylporphyrin (TNPP) and Tetrafluorophenylporphyrin (TFPP) that are scrutinized in this study. Characterization techniques such as LC-MS and 1H NMR were used to confirm the structure of porphyrin derivatives. The mass obtained from LC-MS analysis (Fig. S3) for TAPP (675.65), TMPP (671.65), TFPP (687.65) and TNPP (795.65) are in agreement with their theoretical masses. Further supporting evidences are given in Fig. S4, which shows the 1H NMR spectra of TAPP, TMPP, TFPP & TNPP.

Later, they were subjected to measure the UV–Visible absorbance and steady state fluorescence spectra in acetonitrile solvent, which is given as Fig. 1A &B respectively. They all show the characteristic bands of porphyrins such as Soret band around 410–430 nm and the four Q bands in the range of 500 to 650 nm that arises due to the S0 to S2 and S0 to S1 transitions respectively. These bands are tabulated in Table 1 .

Determination of the Stokes shift is an important parameter in the case of fluorescent molecules. The large shift augments its practical applications like fluorescence imaging because the reabsorption of emitted light is ruled out in such molecules [34]. Also it points out the differences between the structure & properties of ground and excited state porphyrins [35]. The Stokes shift values of four porphyrin molecules were calculated using the Eq. (1) [36].
(1)
S t o k e s
s h i f t =
ϑ a
m a x
−
ϑ f
m a x
where
ϑ a
m a x
is the wavenumber corresponding to the maximum absorption wavelength and
ϑ f
m a x
is the wavenumber corresponding to the maximum emission wavelength. Herein, we have calculated the shift as 531.64 cm- 1 (24 nm), 189.94 cm- 1 (8 nm), 143.78 cm- 1 (6 nm) & 191.12 cm- 1 (8 nm) for TAPP, TMPP, TFPP and TNPP respectively, for Q(0,0) transitions of porphyrins in acetonitrile solvent (Table 1). Thusly, the least overlap between the absorption and emission spectra is observed in aminoporphyrin compared to the remaining three.

From earlier days itself the good light harvesting property of porphyrin molecules is known that reflects in their molar extinction coefficient (MEC) values. Ergo, in our systems we have calculated the MEC values and are tabulated in Table S1, corresponding to both Soret and Q bands absorption maxima. All the four derivatives manifest high MEC values such as 1.18×10 5 M−1cm−1, 6.10×105 M−1cm−1, 4.08×104 M−1cm−1 & 1.06×105 M−1cm−1 for TAPP, TMPP, TNPP & TFPP respectively at their Soret bands. This reckons their higher light harvesting ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge transfer dynamics between porphyrin derivatives and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To unravel the intermolecular charge transfer dynamics between the PDs and GQD, the change in photophysical properties were monitored by the addition of increasing concentration of GQDs to a fixed concentration of PDs in acetonitrile solvent. Ground state interactions were observed via UV–Visible spectroscopic analysis and the excited state charge transfer dynamics were investigated with the aid of fluorescence spectroscopy and time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique.

UV–Visible absorption studies. The charge transfer dynamics on the electron withdrawing and releasing group substituted porphyrins in the presence of GQDs is discussed in this section. Fig. 2 (A-D) shows the absorption spectra of PDs on the incremental addition of GQDs in the range of 0 to 170 μg. There exists only trivial interactions within this nanohybrids in the ground state.

Steady state luminescence studies and the quenching mechanism. In order to understand their excited state interactions, the changes in steady-state fluorescence intensity of PDs were monitored during the incremental addition of GQDs (from 0 to 170 μg), given as Fig. 3 (A-D). It was noted that there is a regular quenching in its intensity and the corresponding quenching efficiency (QE) was calculated which indicates the extent of charge transfer within these systems. For 170 μg concentration of GQD, the quenching efficiencies of TAPP, TMPP, TNPP and TFPP are deemed to be 28 %, 12 %, 41 % and 39 % respectively. These changes in the QE clearly indicates a stronger interaction between TNPP & GQD, while the least interaction exists between TMPP & GQD.

On appraising the fluorescence quenching behaviour, the impetus for this is generally some of the facts such as aggregation induced quenching (AIQ), molecular rearrangements, Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), ground-state complex formation and collisional quenching/photoinduced electron transfer (PET).

For all the four nanohybrids, the feasibility of AIQ was derelicted because the shape of the peak and emission wavelength is invariant after the addition of higher amounts of GQDs. In parallel, the trivial interactions in the UV–Visible absorption spectrum (Fig. 2(A-D)) of nanohybrids indicate that there is no characteristic ground state complex formation within the hybrids. On evaluating the chances of having FRET mechanism, the overlapping spectra of absorption and emission of PDs and GQDs are plotted and given as Fig. S1. This spectrum portraits that, there is no overlapping between the absorption spectrum of GQD and fluorescence spectrum of porphyrin (Fig. S1(A)), which blocks the feasibility of energy transfer from PDs to GQDs. Hence FRET could not be a reason for the quenching pattern observed for the emission of PDs in the presence of increasing concentration of GQD. Meanwhile, in the obverse case of above, that is in the overlapping spectrum between the absorption of PDs and emission of GQDs, overlapping to a lesser extent was observed (Fig. S1(B)). For the quantitative analysis of the extent of overlap, the overlapping integral was measured and the integrals are 2.97×109 nm4M−1cm−1, 2.40×109 nm4M−1cm−1, 1.19×1010 nm4M−1cm−1 & 8.43×109 nm4M−1cm−1 for TAPP:GQD, TMPP:GQD, TNPP:GQD & TFPP:GQD respectively. Hence the possibility of FRET from GQD to the PDs cannot be discarded [23]. However this will not interfere with the current quenching study of porphyrin.

Time-resolved lifetime studies. To get a clear cut idea on interfacial charge transfer dynamics, steady state lifetime measurements have been analyzed for the four nanohybrid systems and are displayed in Fig. 4 (A-D). In all the cases, a decreasing trend compared to the bare PDs was observed for the decay curve on increasing the concentration of GQDs. The obtained lifetime plots were fitted by the bi-exponential decay Eq. S1 (Section S1) with the lowest χ2 value and their fitting parameters are tabulated in Table S2 to S5 in the supporting information. The results show a decreasing trend in the short lifetime component with respect to the increase in GQD concentration, while the long lifetime remains almost constant. Since the percentage short lifetime contribution is more, a similar trend is followed for the average lifetime. The average lifetime value calculated using the Eq. S2 (Section S1), diminishes on increasing the concentration of GQD from 0 to 170 μg, similar to fluorescence quenching. Along with that there perceived a steady increase in the non-radiative rate constant (knr), which is reckoned by the Eq. S3 (Section S1). For 170 μg concentration of GQD, the knr values are 71.14×109 s−1, 66.92×109 s−1, 32.12×109 s−1 & 19.05×109 s−1 corresponding to TNPP:GQD, TFPP:GQD, TAPP:GQD and TMPP:GQD nanohybrids respectively. Here, the knr refers to the non-radiative electron transfer from photoexcited porphyrin molecules to GQDs that wrestles with the fluorescence of porphyrins. Thusly, the increased knr value represents the augmented electron transfer in the nanohybrids and also it is noted that there is significant difference in their rates. That is, TNPP:GQD>TFPP:GQD>TAPP:GQD>TMPP:GQD, which directly stipulates the enhanced photoexcited electron transfer in porphyrins with electron withdrawing groups than that with electron donating groups. Besides, the change in knr values follow the same trend as that of QE.

Dynamics of photoinduced electron transfer. In PET there are two main ways of possible quenching, such as static and dynamic quenching. Static quenching resulted from a permanent non-fluorescent complex formation, while dynamic quenching is from the collisions of fluorophore and quencher. Static will be evident from the ground state interactions which was found to be absent in our systems (Fig. 2(A-D)), ruling out the chance of static quenching. The evidence for the dynamic quenching of PET was first extracted from the Stern-Volmer (SV) plots (Fig. 5A &B) for fluorescence quenching and steady state decay quenching of PDs in presence of GQD (from 0 to 170 μg). The linear plot for both emission and lifetime in all the cases represents the dynamic nature of the quenching mechanism. The rate of quenching is obtained from the slope of the straight line SV plot, fitted with the Eq. (2) [37].
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K D
[ Q ]
where F0 is the fluorescence intensity without quencher, F is the fluorescence intensity with quencher and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. Associated KD values for TNPP:GQD is 60.02 M−1, TFPP:GQD is 46.33 M−1, TAPP:GQD is 27.15 M−1 and TMPP:GQD is 11.79 M−1. Compared to remaining three systems, the higher KD value associated with TNPP:GQD indicates the stronger interaction within this system and it is least in TMPP:GQD. In general, the porphyrins with EWG showed greater interaction with GQD compared to that with EDG substituents.

As referred before, this photoinduced electron transfer is a thermodynamically feasible one, it is necessary to understand the extent of feasibility. For that the change in Gibbs free energy is calculated with the assistance of the following Eq. 3, known as Rehm-Weller formalism [33].
(3)
Δ
G =
E
o x d
0
(
D
+
D
)
−
E
r e d
0
(
A
A
−
)
−
E
00
s
− C
where
E
o x d
0
is the oxidation potential of the porphyrin donors,
E
r e d
0
is the reduction potential of the acceptor GQD,
E
00
s
is the excitation energy of GQD, which is 2.20 eV (intersection point of absorption & emission spectra of GQD) and C is the coulombic stabilization energy which is neglected in the first approximation [33]. The consequent ∆G values of the PET within the nanohybrid systems are -26.57 kcal/mol, -22.53 kcal/mol, -21.40 kcal/mol & -20.18 kcal/mol respectively for TNPP:GQD, TFPP:GQD, TAPP:GQD & TMPP:GQD. These high negative ∆G affirms the efficient PET process from porphyrin donors to GQD acceptor and the differences in their rate of electron transfer. As said before, herein the TNPP:GQD system shows enhanced feasibility of electron transfer and it is least in the case of TMPP:GQD.

Cyclic Voltammetric Studies. Cyclic Voltammetric studies were done to address the above depicted routes of charge transfer dynamics within the nanohybrid systems. The detailed procedure and the calculations are given in the supporting information (Section S2). Fig. S5 portrays the Cyclic Voltammogram of TAPP, TMPP, TFPP, TNPP & GQD. The electronic energy levels of the individual systems were calculated from their Voltammograms and optical bandgaps. The highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels for the parent systems were calculated from their corresponding first oxidation potentials using the Eq. S4 [38]. While the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) were obtained by the addition of optical band gap (Eg) with EHOMO, where Eg were obtained from the intersection point of their absorption and emission spectra.

The HOMOs, LUMOs and their optical bandgaps are tabulated in Table 2 and parallelly their energy level diagram is portrayed which is given as Fig. S6. For all the nanohybrid systems, the LUMOs of PDs always lie above the LUMO of GQD (-3.08 eV) that allows a thermodynamically feasible PET from PDs to GQD, irrespective of their electron withdrawing/releasing substituents. This is antithetical to the conventional smaller electron withdrawing/releasing systems as reported earlier [39]. That is the aromatic electron rich porphines dominate over its substituents, either electron withdrawing or releasing and perpetually act as an electron donor along with GQD molecules.
All the experimental studies given above show the order of interactions within the nanohybrids as TNPP:GQD>TFPP:GQD>TAPP:GQD>TMPP:GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Computational studies on the interactions within GQD-porphyrin nanohybrid systems
ce:section.ce:para: For an in-depth knowledge of the interactions within the porphyrin-GQD nanohybrids, we have performed the computational calculations using the hybrid method of CAM-B3LYP and 6-31G(d,p) basis set included in the Gaussian 09 program package. At first, we have carried out the optimization study of the porphyrin derivatives (TNPP, TFPP, TAPP & TMPP) and GQDs. From these optimized geometries (Fig. S7) given in the supporting information, the frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of each structure (Fig. 6 ) and their respective energies were taken for the calculation of their bandgap. The order of FMO energy levels obtained from this calculation is found to be in good agreement with that of the experimentally obtained values. Herein, the HOMO (−6.37 eV) and LUMO (−1.72 eV) levels of GQD occupy a lower energy than the respective energy levels of porphyrin derivatives, which indicates the feasible PET from porphyrins to GQDs.

Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP) surface analysis depicts the 3-D charge distribution images with different colours and are shown as Fig. S8. This diagram help to understand the electrophilic and nucleophilic attacking sites of molecules and thereby impart an idea of charge distribution within the molecule [40,41]. The electrostatic potential varies from a highly negative potential (deep red) to highly positive potential (deep blue), which simultaneously represents the fierce repulsion and attraction sites [42,43]. As evident from the figure, GQDs have more positive potential that favours their electron withdrawing nature. Meanwhile passing through the PDs, three of them (TAPP, TMPP & TFPP) have high negative potential inside their core, whereas in TNPP the porphyrin core exhibits a slight positive potential. This is due to the high electron withdrawing nature of the nitro groups at the periphery. Therefore the phenyl ends of TNPP become comparatively more attractive towards the GQDs. These results ensure the degree of interactions between the PDs and GQDs.

The FMOs corresponding to the GQD nanohybrids with the PDs are portrayed in Fig. 7 , which unveils the charge localizations within them. As shown in the figure, the HOMO and LUMO levels are concentrated on PDs and GQDs respectively, which is clearly in agreement with the observed electron transfer from individual FMO orbitals (Fig. 6). As said before, this is a symbol of electron donating nature of porphyrins and accepting nature of GQDs, in porphyrin:GQD hybrid systems. That is, the PET from PDs to GQDs is confirmed once again. Moving to the types of interactions within these hybrids, there exists three possible ways such as hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and weak Van der Waals interactions, manifested from scatter plots of reduced density gradient (Fig. S9). This leads to the strong binding and co-facial orientation of these hybrid components.

Shubin Liu's decomposition analysis helps to quantify the total interaction energy, from which the electrostatic component and (steric+quantum) components are extracted with the aid of extralink calculations. The corresponding graphical representation is given as Fig. 8 , in which all the nanohybrids follow the same trend in case of all the energies. Among them the electrostatic potential energy is the one that stabilizes these nanohybrid systems, whereas the steric and quantum energies destabilize. It is manifested from the figure that the stabilizing energy dominates over the destabilizing ones that favours the formation of all the four systems. Also, the TNPP:GQD hybrid shows comparatively high values of electrostatic energy, which is evident in the MEP surfaces. This indicates the strong interactions between TNPP and GQD in comparison with remaining three hybrid systems. The order of energy from this decomposition analysis is TNPP:GQD>TFPP:GQD>TAPP:GQD>TMPP:GQD, which follows the same order as that in the experimental observations. Also, it is heeded that, the electron withdrawing group substituted porphyrins have greater interactions with GQD than the electron releasing group substituted porphyrins. All the above computational calculation were also performed in the acetonitrile medium and the corresponding data are incorporated in the supporting information (Table S6 & S7), which exhibits the same trend as that of the calculations in vacuum.

Main aim of this study is to understand the electronic and spectroscopic changes within porphyrin- graphene quantum dot nanohybrids, by varying the substituents in the meso position of porphyrins. Four of the porphyrin derivatives substituted with electron withdrawing groups (-NO2 & -F) and electron releasing groups (-NH2 & -CH3) are synthesized by the conventional methods. Photophysical studies of the nanohybrid systems based on these porphyrins and GQDs reflect that there exists a strong binding within the nanohybrid system. UV–Visible spectroscopic studies indicate that there is no characteristic ground state interactions, but an effective excited state interaction is observed from fluorescence spectroscopy. This excited state interactions are further supported by the steady state lifetime analysis, in which a decrease in the average lifetime value and a hike in the non-radiative rate constants are observed on the incremental addition of GQDs to the porphyrins. This stipulates that the charge transfer dynamics within the nanohybrids follow a photoinduced electron transfer with dynamic quenching mechanism. Gibb's free energy values, Stern-Volmer plots and electronic energy levels are in support to this charge transfer dynamics.

Further the computational calculations were used to substantiate these experimental observations. FMO levels and MEP surface analysis of the individual systems before their hybrid formation show the feasibility of excited state electron transfer from porphyrin molecules to GQDs, which are further confirmed from the optimized geometries of their nanohybrids. The observed modes of interactions are hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking and weak Van der Waals interactions that are manifested from scatter plots of reduced density gradient, indicating a co-facial orientation of hybrid components. The energy decomposition analysis helps to quantify the interaction energies and it is found that the higher energy is possessed by the TNPP:GQD system. Also, it is heeded that, the electron withdrawing group substituted porphyrins have greater interactions with GQD than the electron releasing group substituted porphyrins in both experimental and computational calculations.

Athira Pallikkara: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Pothoppurathu M Vijay: Visualization, Resources, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kala Ramakrishnan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization. Elambalassery G Jayasree: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Software, Investigation, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Cochin University of Science and Technology (CUSAT) for the support through SMNRI Project and STIC & Department of Physics for the analysis. We also acknowledge DST-SERB for the financial support (EMR/2016/005601). The partial financial support obtained from TDP/BDTD/34/2021, UGC-SAP, DST-FIST, DST-PURSE, PLEASE and RUSA projects are also acknowledged. The authors would also like to thank the computer center at the Center of Integrated Studies, CUSAT for providing the computational facility.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.138686.
